subject: english
topic_title: Summary Writing, Adjectival Clause, and Films and TV Vocabulary
learning_objectives: Define vocabulary associated with films and TV

sections.heading: SUMMARY: Agriculture Technical Aid to Africa
sections.content: The agriculture technical aid scheme is one of the measures designed by African experts to address food security issues in Africa. Under the scheme, trained Nigerians would be sent to other African countries who require their services in cassava tech-nology. Egyptians could help out in fish production.

sections.heading: Structure: Adjectival Clause
sections.content: An adjectival clause performs the functions of an adjective i.e. it qualifies a noun or its equivalents. An adjectival clause begins with a relative pronoun such as who, which, that, whose, where, whom etc. hence it is called a (defining) relative clause as well. This clause is not separated by a comma from the noun it describes. But when the clause is marked off by a comma from the noun it describes, it is not a relative clause. Examples: The man whose car was stolen has reported the case. The boy whom you praised so well has been rewarded. I will retire to a city where the standard of living is low. The man I met told me the secret. Dayo, who returned from London yesterday has returned. Things fall apart, which was written by Chinua Achebe, is a best seller.

sections.heading: Vocabulary Development: Words Associated with Films and TV
sections.content: Slanting the news – expressing the news in a biased or prejudiced way so as to mislead the public censor – to approve whatever is published or broadcast before it is made public. Curtail – restrict. Director – A person who directs the performance of a movie. Producer – a person who has general control especially of the money for a play, film or broadcast, but doesn’t direct actors. Box-office – office where seats in the theatre or hall are booked and tickets are paid for. Rehearse – to learn and practise something for later performance. Star – a famous or very skilful performer. Audience – the people listening to or watching a performance. Curtain call – call made by the audience to an actor or actress at the end of the play to acknowledge applause.
